top_strategic.title: Sponsorship whitespace: 4 equipment categories with ZERO brand detection
top_strategic.evidence_summary: The camera sees these items but we detect no brands. ZERO detections: accessories, apparel, footwear, wristband. Near-zero: sunglasses(2). Each gap = a brand that could be paying for visibility data.
top_strategic.recommended_action: Add shoe/apparel/sunglasses brand detection examples to Gemini prompt — immediate revenue unlock
top_strategic.plausibility_note: Zero-detection insight is quick to surface; prototype is a filtered view + outreach template, but validation with brands takes time.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Confirmation that the detection algorithm was tested against ground truth (manual annotation of 100+ clips) showing false negative rate <5% for these categories
multi_model_check.blind_spot: No distinction between 'camera can't see the brand' vs 'brand exists but detection model failed' — low detection could mean poor model performance, not market opportunity

follow_up.follow_up_question: Who is the first buyer? POC in 1 week?
follow_up.proposed_action: Add shoe/apparel/sunglasses brand detection examples to Gemini prompt — immediate revenue unlock

top_strategic.title: Sponsorship whitespace detected in 3288 clips (96.3%) — sell to brands
top_strategic.evidence_summary: We detect unbranded equipment/space in 3288 clips. This is the gap between what's visible and what's branded — sellable data.
top_strategic.recommended_action: Package sponsorship whitespace data as 'brand opportunity report' for equipment companies

source_clips.summary: A 17-shot driving rally ends with an unforced error into the net.
Players engage in a prolonged kitchen dink rally, demonstrating patience and control.
Players engage in a consistent rally of dinks at the kitchen line.

top_strategic.plausibility_note: Data already exists and labeled; needs simple brand matching API and sales dashboard, but GTM validation is the bottleneck.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Evidence that brands actually pay premium rates for unbranded equipment footage, or testimonial from a brand that purchased this data and activated it
multi_model_check.blind_spot: Assumes unbranded = sellable. Brands may prefer NOT to see competitor equipment. No validation that this gap is actually monetizable or that buyers exist

follow_up.follow_up_question: Who is the first buyer? POC in 1 week?
follow_up.proposed_action: Package sponsorship whitespace data as 'brand opportunity report' for equipment companies

top_strategic.title: Idea Lab as a White-Label SaaS
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Licensing the Idea Lab itself to adjacent sports (tennis, padel, pickleball leagues, badminton, bowling) and non-sports (fashion retail, restaurant serviceware, retail shelf analytics). A paddle tennis facility buys 'Paddle DaaS' with the same Idea Lab framework. An athletic apparel retailer buys 'Fit DaaS' analyzing video of products in the wild. We become the underlying research OS, not the pickleball company. Each vertical's Idea Lab feeds insights into a shared model — massive network effects.
top_strategic.plausibility_note: White-label SaaS requires auth, multi-tenancy, billing infrastructure—typical SaaS complexity compresses poorly into 30 days solo.

counterargument.argument: White-labeling the Idea Lab assumes these adjacent sports have the same clip volume, annotation infrastructure, and willingness to pay as pickleball—they don't.
counterargument.evidence: Tennis and badminton have established analytics vendors (Hawk-Eye, Synergy Sports); retail/fashion use computer vision for completely different tasks (SKU detection, not human micro-actions). You'd need to rebuild training data for each sport, retrain models, and sell to buyers who already have solutions. The 'platform play' framing masks that you have zero product-market fit signals outside pickleball, and pivoting dilutes the core DaaS business.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Signed letters of intent or discovery calls with 2+ sports leagues/brands expressing interest in licensing, with pricing discussion
multi_model_check.blind_spot: SaaS licensing requires: (1) replicable tech stack documentation, (2) proof the model works on non-pickleball sports, (3) actual customer acquisition strategy. None provided. Assumes 'adjacent sports' want this tool without validation

follow_up.follow_up_question: Smallest experiment to test this in 30 days?
follow_up.proposed_action: Evaluate: platform_play play

top_strategic.title: Crowdsourced Coaching Bounty Board
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Reverse the supply chain: post clips anonymously from the Courtana corpus on a bounty board. Coaches bid to analyze them and provide feedback. Best feedback is algorithmically scored by the Idea Lab and gets paid. Coaches build portfolios. The Courtana player eventually gets the feedback for free or low-cost (paid by brand sponsorships). Coaches compete to be better, making the product better. Brands get to sponsor coaching feedback. Creates a gig-economy moat and crowdsources improvement.
top_strategic.plausibility_note: Crowdsourced board is a task posting + review UI with basic payment splits; straightforward to build, but coach adoption and quality control is uncertain.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Proof of concept with 20+ coaches actively bidding on 50+ clips, with completion rates >60% and creators willing to pay for feedback
multi_model_check.blind_spot: Inverts the actual market dynamic: coaches are commoditized, not scarce. No evidence players value external coaching feedback on video, or would trust anonymous analysis. Ignores existing platforms (Hudl, Wistia) solving this

follow_up.follow_up_question: Smallest experiment to test this in 30 days?
follow_up.proposed_action: Evaluate: crowdsource play

top_strategic.title: Endorsement intelligence: 164 player-brand pairs with 3+ co-occurrences
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Top pairs: PickleBill-JOOLA(913x), Chintan-JOOLA(293x), Tmf-JOOLA(244x), Ironvarr-JOOLA(205x), Santinofuntila-JOOLA(161x)
top_strategic.recommended_action: Build 'brand ambassador identification' product for equipment companies
top_strategic.plausibility_note: Data is pre-identified; prototype requires basic database + matching UI, but proving monetization to brands in 30 days is tight.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Confirmation these 164 pairs represent VOLUNTARY endorsements (contracts/explicit partnerships) vs. coincidental co-occurrence; validation that co-occurrence frequency correlates with actual sponsorship deals
multi_model_check.blind_spot: Co-occurrence ≠ endorsement. A player appearing with JOOLA 913 times could mean: (1) they own one paddle, (2) random chance, (3) JOOLA dominates the tournament. No signal that these are paid partnerships or exploitable intelligence

follow_up.follow_up_question: Who is the first buyer? POC in 1 week?
follow_up.proposed_action: Build 'brand ambassador identification' product for equipment companies

top_data.title: Prompt fix: 5 badges consistently missed — add positive examples
top_data.evidence_summary: Production awards these but DaaS misses them: 'rally master' (6 missed), 'new look' (5 missed), 'steady eddie' (4 missed), 'fresh fit' (3 missed), 'paddle tap celebration' (3 missed). These are real badges that our prompt doesn't detect.
top_data.recommended_action:
Add positive examples to Gemini prompt:
'When analyzing badges, make sure to check for these commonly missed badges:
  - rally master: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - new look: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - steady eddie: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - fresh fit: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - paddle tap celebration: Look for [describe visual pattern]

counterargument.argument: Adding 5 specific positive examples to a prompt does not systematically fix recall; it's a band-aid that will mask the underlying model limitation and create false confidence.
counterargument.evidence: You're treating symptoms (5 missed badges) rather than the root cause (Gemini's visual understanding is unreliable on subtle actions like 'rally master'). Adding examples to a prompt inflates F1 on your test set but does nothing for the 50 other badges you haven't caught yet. Once deployed, customers will report new misses weekly, forcing perpetual prompt tweaks. You lack a way to validate whether the fix actually generalizes to unseen clips or just overfits to your 21 examples.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Can we A/B test this on 50 clips?
follow_up.proposed_action:
Add positive examples to Gemini prompt:
'When analyzing badges, make sure to check for these commonly missed badges:
  - rally master: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - new look: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - steady eddie: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - fresh fit: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - paddle tap celebration: Look for [describe visual pattern]

top_data.title: DaaS badge prediction: F1=0.98 (precision=0.99, recall=0.98)
top_data.evidence_summary: Across 460 verified clips: 1821 correct predictions, 16 false positives, 41 missed badges. Overall F1=0.985.
top_data.recommended_action: Target recall improvement: add more badge examples to Gemini prompt

counterargument.argument: An F1=0.98 on 460 clips is statistically fragile when deployed against millions of real-world clips with distribution drift.
counterargument.evidence: The test set of 460 clips is likely curated, well-lit, and unrepresentative of production footage (low angles, motion blur, outdoor glare, night courts). The 16 false positives and 41 misses suggest the model is still making errors at scale—extrapolated to 1M clips, that's 34,700+ false positives creating noise for customers. Gemini's vision model degrades unpredictably on edge cases, and you have no holdout test set to validate generalization.

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the ROI of fixing this? How many clips affected?
follow_up.proposed_action: Target recall improvement: add more badge examples to Gemini prompt

top_20.title: Net/venue branding inflates 7 brands — true share-of-court differs from raw counts
top_20.evidence_summary: Brands with significant venue-only detections (not product use): JOOLA (446/5638 are venue), Nicol (6/14 are venue), PUSHIT (5/9 are venue), adidas (4/47 are venue), Onix (3/38 are venue). Raw counts overstate market share. Product-use only: JOOLA=5192, Nike=566, PICKLEBALL=171, HEAD=101, Reolink=98.
top_20.recommended_action: Report TRUE share-of-court (product-use only) to brand partners, not raw detection counts

counterargument.argument: Venue-only detections are a data quality issue, but the fix (reporting 'true' product-use) assumes you can reliably distinguish venue branding from product use—you likely can't.
counterargument.evidence: A JOOLA net and a JOOLA paddle look visually similar to a model; you're conflating two detection problems. If you report only 'true' product-use counts to brands, you'll miss sales attribution (a player practicing at a JOOLA venue *is* exposed to the brand). Brands care about total impression share, not just product-use; filtering out venue data may actually harm the value of your dataset. You also have no way to validate this correction without manually reviewing thousands of clips—operationally infeasible.

top_20.title: Viral formula: quality (7.41 vs 4.74) + watchability (6.79 vs 2.78)
top_20.evidence_summary: Top 100 viral clips: quality=7.41, watchability=6.79. Bottom 100: quality=4.74, watchability=2.78. Both metrics track strongly with viral potential.
top_20.recommended_action: Use (quality + watchability) / 2 as quick viral prediction score

counterargument.argument: A simple (quality + watchability) / 2 score assumes these two metrics are equally weighted, linearly additive, and predictive of virality—none of which you've validated.
counterargument.evidence: You've identified a correlation (top 100 viral clips score higher) but not causation or weighting. Virality also depends on timing, distribution platform, hashtags, and luck—factors your metrics don't capture. A clip could be high-quality but not watchable (slow rally) or watchable but low-quality (blurry but dramatic). Using this score to pitch 'viral prediction' to brands or players sets false expectations; you'll be wrong 40% of the time, damaging credibility. This needs validation on a held-out set and comparison to a null model (e.g., random clips go viral at X% baseline rate).

top_20.title: Rally length matters: long rallies are more viral (long=3.1 vs short=2.2)
top_20.evidence_summary: Avg rally: 15.7 shots. Long rallies (15+): 1758 clips, avg viral 3.1. Short (4-): 58 clips, avg viral 2.2.
top_20.recommended_action: Use rally length as a quality signal in highlight curation

top_20.title: Prompt fix: JOOLA appears in 338/460 verified clips — add disambiguation
top_20.evidence_summary: JOOLA dominates 73% of clips. Prompt should distinguish: paddle logo (real JOOLA use), net logo (JOOLA sponsors nets), and venue branding (JOOLA sponsors the facility).
top_20.recommended_action:
Add to brand detection prompt:
'For JOOLA specifically, classify detection source:
  - paddle: JOOLA logo on paddle face = confirmed product use
  - net: JOOLA logo on net = venue sponsorship, NOT product use
  - venue: JOOLA on walls/banners = facility sponsorship
Only count paddle detections as brand_category: paddle'

top_20.evidence_summary: Story arc 'athletic' appears in 21.0% of top-viral clips but only 0.0% of bottom-viral clips.
top_20.recommended_action: Prioritize 'athletic' clips in automated highlight reels

all_findings.title: Viral shot: 'overhead' clips average 5.12 viral vs 'none' at 1
all_findings.evidence_summary: Shot viral averages: overhead=5.12, speed_up=4.27, reset=3.91, volley=3.58, dink=2.98, drive=2.89, serve=2.18, unknown=1.2, none=1

follow_up.follow_up_question: Does this hold across different skill levels?
follow_up.proposed_action: Prioritize highlight reels featuring high-viral shot types

all_findings.title: Prompt fix: 5 badges consistently missed — add positive examples
all_findings.evidence_summary: Production awards these but DaaS misses them: 'rally master' (6 missed), 'new look' (5 missed), 'steady eddie' (4 missed), 'fresh fit' (3 missed), 'paddle tap celebration' (3 missed). These are real badges that our prompt doesn't detect.
all_findings.recommended_action:
Add positive examples to Gemini prompt:
'When analyzing badges, make sure to check for these commonly missed badges:
  - rally master: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - new look: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - steady eddie: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - fresh fit: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - paddle tap celebration: Look for [describe visual pattern]

counterargument.argument: Adding 5 specific positive examples to a prompt does not systematically fix recall; it's a band-aid that will mask the underlying model limitation and create false confidence.
counterargument.evidence: You're treating symptoms (5 missed badges) rather than the root cause (Gemini's visual understanding is unreliable on subtle actions like 'rally master'). Adding examples to a prompt inflates F1 on your test set but does nothing for the 50 other badges you haven't caught yet. Once deployed, customers will report new misses weekly, forcing perpetual prompt tweaks. You lack a way to validate whether the fix actually generalizes to unseen clips or just overfits to your 21 examples.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Can we A/B test this on 50 clips?
follow_up.proposed_action:
Add positive examples to Gemini prompt:
'When analyzing badges, make sure to check for these commonly missed badges:
  - rally master: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - new look: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - steady eddie: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - fresh fit: Look for [describe visual pattern]
  - paddle tap celebration: Look for [describe visual pattern]

all_findings.title: DaaS badge prediction: F1=0.98 (precision=0.99, recall=0.98)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Across 460 verified clips: 1821 correct predictions, 16 false positives, 41 missed badges. Overall F1=0.985.
all_findings.recommended_action: Target recall improvement: add more badge examples to Gemini prompt

counterargument.argument: An F1=0.98 on 460 clips is statistically fragile when deployed against millions of real-world clips with distribution drift.
counterargument.evidence: The test set of 460 clips is likely curated, well-lit, and unrepresentative of production footage (low angles, motion blur, outdoor glare, night courts). The 16 false positives and 41 misses suggest the model is still making errors at scale—extrapolated to 1M clips, that's 34,700+ false positives creating noise for customers. Gemini's vision model degrades unpredictably on edge cases, and you have no holdout test set to validate generalization.

follow_up.follow_up_question: What's the ROI of fixing this? How many clips affected?
follow_up.proposed_action: Target recall improvement: add more badge examples to Gemini prompt

all_findings.evidence_summary: Lowest F1 badges: third shot drop artist (F1=0.955), hustler (F1=0.919). These need prompt improvement.
all_findings.recommended_action: Add specific examples for these badge types in Gemini prompt

follow_up.proposed_action: Add specific examples for these badge types in Gemini prompt

all_findings.title: Net/venue branding inflates 7 brands — true share-of-court differs from raw counts
all_findings.evidence_summary: Brands with significant venue-only detections (not product use): JOOLA (446/5638 are venue), Nicol (6/14 are venue), PUSHIT (5/9 are venue), adidas (4/47 are venue), Onix (3/38 are venue). Raw counts overstate market share. Product-use only: JOOLA=5192, Nike=566, PICKLEBALL=171, HEAD=101, Reolink=98.
all_findings.recommended_action: Report TRUE share-of-court (product-use only) to brand partners, not raw detection counts

counterargument.argument: Venue-only detections are a data quality issue, but the fix (reporting 'true' product-use) assumes you can reliably distinguish venue branding from product use—you likely can't.
counterargument.evidence: A JOOLA net and a JOOLA paddle look visually similar to a model; you're conflating two detection problems. If you report only 'true' product-use counts to brands, you'll miss sales attribution (a player practicing at a JOOLA venue *is* exposed to the brand). Brands care about total impression share, not just product-use; filtering out venue data may actually harm the value of your dataset. You also have no way to validate this correction without manually reviewing thousands of clips—operationally infeasible.

all_findings.title: Viral formula: quality (7.41 vs 4.74) + watchability (6.79 vs 2.78)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Top 100 viral clips: quality=7.41, watchability=6.79. Bottom 100: quality=4.74, watchability=2.78. Both metrics track strongly with viral potential.
all_findings.recommended_action: Use (quality + watchability) / 2 as quick viral prediction score

counterargument.argument: A simple (quality + watchability) / 2 score assumes these two metrics are equally weighted, linearly additive, and predictive of virality—none of which you've validated.
counterargument.evidence: You've identified a correlation (top 100 viral clips score higher) but not causation or weighting. Virality also depends on timing, distribution platform, hashtags, and luck—factors your metrics don't capture. A clip could be high-quality but not watchable (slow rally) or watchable but low-quality (blurry but dramatic). Using this score to pitch 'viral prediction' to brands or players sets false expectations; you'll be wrong 40% of the time, damaging credibility. This needs validation on a held-out set and comparison to a null model (e.g., random clips go viral at X% baseline rate).

all_findings.title: Rally length matters: long rallies are more viral (long=3.1 vs short=2.2)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Avg rally: 15.7 shots. Long rallies (15+): 1758 clips, avg viral 3.1. Short (4-): 58 clips, avg viral 2.2.
all_findings.recommended_action: Use rally length as a quality signal in highlight curation

all_findings.title: Prompt fix: JOOLA appears in 338/460 verified clips — add disambiguation
all_findings.evidence_summary: JOOLA dominates 73% of clips. Prompt should distinguish: paddle logo (real JOOLA use), net logo (JOOLA sponsors nets), and venue branding (JOOLA sponsors the facility).
all_findings.recommended_action:
Add to brand detection prompt:
'For JOOLA specifically, classify detection source:
  - paddle: JOOLA logo on paddle face = confirmed product use
  - net: JOOLA logo on net = venue sponsorship, NOT product use
  - venue: JOOLA on walls/banners = facility sponsorship
Only count paddle detections as brand_category: paddle'

all_findings.title: Viral arc: 'athletic' is 210.0x more likely in top clips (21.0% vs 0.0%)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Story arc 'athletic' appears in 21.0% of top-viral clips but only 0.0% of bottom-viral clips.
all_findings.recommended_action: Prioritize 'athletic' clips in automated highlight reels

all_findings.title: Sponsorship whitespace: 4 equipment categories with ZERO brand detection
all_findings.evidence_summary: The camera sees these items but we detect no brands. ZERO detections: accessories, apparel, footwear, wristband. Near-zero: sunglasses(2). Each gap = a brand that could be paying for visibility data.
all_findings.recommended_action: Add shoe/apparel/sunglasses brand detection examples to Gemini prompt — immediate revenue unlock
all_findings.plausibility_note: Zero-detection insight is quick to surface; prototype is a filtered view + outreach template, but validation with brands takes time.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Confirmation that the detection algorithm was tested against ground truth (manual annotation of 100+ clips) showing false negative rate <5% for these categories
multi_model_check.blind_spot: No distinction between 'camera can't see the brand' vs 'brand exists but detection model failed' — low detection could mean poor model performance, not market opportunity

follow_up.follow_up_question: Who is the first buyer? POC in 1 week?
follow_up.proposed_action: Add shoe/apparel/sunglasses brand detection examples to Gemini prompt — immediate revenue unlock

all_findings.title: Pickleball Fantasy League (Powered by Real Data)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Launch a B2C fantasy pickleball app where casual fans draft real players from Courtana's network. Daily/weekly scoring based on actual Courtana clip performance: shots made, viral moments, skill ratings. Fans pay entry fees ($5-20), we take rake. Brands sponsor fantasy teams and micro-moments ('JOOLA Power Shot of the Week'). Players earn bonus prize pools for being drafted. Creates a viral consumer loop and a direct connection to casual fans who want gear their drafted players use.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: b2c_product play
all_findings.plausibility_note: Fantasy league requires game engine, scoring logic, leaderboards, and player engagement loops—feature-rich and requires user testing feedback.

all_findings.title: Arc-specific prompt issue: 'strategic' clips cause 94% of false positives
all_findings.evidence_summary: The 'strategic' arc type produces disproportionate badge false positives (15/16 = 94%). The prompt may be misinterpreting this arc type's visual patterns.
all_findings.recommended_action: Add arc-specific badge guidance: 'For strategic clips, badge X is common but Y is not'

all_findings.title: Idea Lab as a White-Label SaaS
all_findings.evidence_summary: Licensing the Idea Lab itself to adjacent sports (tennis, padel, pickleball leagues, badminton, bowling) and non-sports (fashion retail, restaurant serviceware, retail shelf analytics). A paddle tennis facility buys 'Paddle DaaS' with the same Idea Lab framework. An athletic apparel retailer buys 'Fit DaaS' analyzing video of products in the wild. We become the underlying research OS, not the pickleball company. Each vertical's Idea Lab feeds insights into a shared model — massive network effects.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: platform_play play
all_findings.plausibility_note: White-label SaaS requires auth, multi-tenancy, billing infrastructure—typical SaaS complexity compresses poorly into 30 days solo.

counterargument.argument: White-labeling the Idea Lab assumes these adjacent sports have the same clip volume, annotation infrastructure, and willingness to pay as pickleball—they don't.
counterargument.evidence: Tennis and badminton have established analytics vendors (Hawk-Eye, Synergy Sports); retail/fashion use computer vision for completely different tasks (SKU detection, not human micro-actions). You'd need to rebuild training data for each sport, retrain models, and sell to buyers who already have solutions. The 'platform play' framing masks that you have zero product-market fit signals outside pickleball, and pivoting dilutes the core DaaS business.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Signed letters of intent or discovery calls with 2+ sports leagues/brands expressing interest in licensing, with pricing discussion
multi_model_check.blind_spot: SaaS licensing requires: (1) replicable tech stack documentation, (2) proof the model works on non-pickleball sports, (3) actual customer acquisition strategy. None provided. Assumes 'adjacent sports' want this tool without validation

follow_up.follow_up_question: Smallest experiment to test this in 30 days?
follow_up.proposed_action: Evaluate: platform_play play

all_findings.title: Prompt fix: Quality scores too clustered (avg=6.5, std=0.7) — increase spread
all_findings.evidence_summary: Quality scores cluster around 6.5 with std=0.7. Scores should use the full 1-10 range. Prompt may need calibration examples.
all_findings.recommended_action:
Add quality calibration to prompt:
'Quality score examples: 1-2 = unwatchable, 3-4 = poor angles/dark, 5-6 = standard gameplay, 7-8 = exciting with good camera, 9-10 = broadcast quality'

all_findings.title: Crowdsourced Coaching Bounty Board
all_findings.evidence_summary: Reverse the supply chain: post clips anonymously from the Courtana corpus on a bounty board. Coaches bid to analyze them and provide feedback. Best feedback is algorithmically scored by the Idea Lab and gets paid. Coaches build portfolios. The Courtana player eventually gets the feedback for free or low-cost (paid by brand sponsorships). Coaches compete to be better, making the product better. Brands get to sponsor coaching feedback. Creates a gig-economy moat and crowdsources improvement.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: crowdsource play
all_findings.plausibility_note: Crowdsourced board is a task posting + review UI with basic payment splits; straightforward to build, but coach adoption and quality control is uncertain.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Proof of concept with 20+ coaches actively bidding on 50+ clips, with completion rates >60% and creators willing to pay for feedback
multi_model_check.blind_spot: Inverts the actual market dynamic: coaches are commoditized, not scarce. No evidence players value external coaching feedback on video, or would trust anonymous analysis. Ignores existing platforms (Hudl, Wistia) solving this

follow_up.follow_up_question: Smallest experiment to test this in 30 days?
follow_up.proposed_action: Evaluate: crowdsource play

all_findings.title: Player-to-Brand Sponsorship Auction
all_findings.evidence_summary: Players with Courtana accounts become 'micro-sponsorable.' Our Idea Lab identifies which players generate the most viral moments with specific gear (Nike player with sick serves, JOOLA player with smash winners). Brands bid to sponsor those players directly in a weekly auction. Player gets paid ($50-500/week), brand gets authentic micro-influencer footage, we take 20% and own the supply-side data. Creates a flywheel: popular players earn more, attract more players to Courtana, makes data more valuable.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: creator_economy play
all_findings.plausibility_note: Auction mechanics require real-time bidding logic, payment processing, and brand legal review; too much complexity for prototype validation.

counterargument.argument: Micro-sponsorship auctions assume players have personal brands worth monetizing and that brands will bid on sub-1K-follower players—neither is true in pickleball.
counterargument.evidence: Pickleball's creator economy is nascent; most players have no social presence. Brands optimize for macro-influencers and established athletes with legal reps; they won't set up auctions to bid on randomized micro-moments from unknown players. Even if they did, the unit economics are terrible (a brand pays $50 to sponsor a 10-second clip from a player with 200 followers?). This is a creator-economy fantasy, not a revenue model, and it cannibalizes direct sponsorship relationships Courtana could negotiate with brands instead.

all_findings.title: Coaching feedback covers 99.9% of clips — drill library is buildable now
all_findings.evidence_summary: Coaching analysis available for 3413/3415 clips.
all_findings.recommended_action: Build 'AI Coaching Library' — categorized drills from real game footage
all_findings.plausibility_note: 99.9% coverage means taxonomy is solid; prototype is searchable drill library UI + tagging—lowest technical complexity on this list.

all_findings.title: Facility Equity Marketplace
all_findings.evidence_summary: Courtana facilities generate clips. Instead of selling data TO them, we let them OWN their data and tokenize it. A facility gets a % ownership in a 'pickleball content fund' that aggregates their best clips. Brands buy stakes in the fund. Facilities earn recurring revenue. As more facilities join, fund becomes more valuable (network effect). The Idea Lab surfaces which facilities' footage is most valuable to different brands, creating a matching marketplace.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: network_effect play
all_findings.plausibility_note: Needs marketplace transactions, escrow, dispute resolution, regulatory review—legal and backend complexity far exceeds 30-day solo scope.

counterargument.argument: A 'facility equity marketplace' requires solving the chicken-egg problem: facilities won't tokenize data without a liquid buyer base, and sponsors won't buy without proof of ROI—neither exists.
counterargument.evidence: You're proposing a two-sided marketplace (supply: facilities, demand: sponsors) with zero network effects in place. Tokenization adds legal/tax complexity without adding value—facilities care about revenue, not ownership %. Even if you solve that, a 'pickleball content fund' is illiquid and speculative; sponsors want guaranteed impressions or player endorsements, not equity. You'd need to operate the marketplace (legal, settlement, disputes) while bootstrapping DaaS—this dilutes focus and has high operational risk for a 1-person team.

all_findings.title: Insurance Product for Equipment Brands
all_findings.evidence_summary: JOOLA, Nike, etc. have product liability and reputation risk. We offer 'Viral Risk Insurance' — we monitor every instance their gear appears in clips, predict reputational damage (player injury, embarrassment, durability fail), and alert them proactively. They pay annual premiums ($50K-200K). We become their early-warning system. The Idea Lab becomes a risk engine. Defensible because we have the only dataset and the predictive model trained on 3415 clips.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: monetization_novel play
all_findings.plausibility_note: Insurance product needs actuarial modeling, underwriting logic, regulatory approval, and legal structure—not a 30-day prototype scope.

all_findings.title: Brand Performance Betting Exchange
all_findings.evidence_summary: Brands pay to submit their own product footage from influencers, athletes, and sponsored events. The Idea Lab analyzes it against Courtana's corpus, generates a 'virality score' and 'engagement multiplier.' Brands then BET on their own clips — essentially buying prediction insurance on whether their sponsored content will go viral. We take a rake on the betting pool and sell the aggregated prediction data back to sponsors and media buyers.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: monetization_novel play
all_findings.plausibility_note: Betting exchange requires financial regulation compliance, KYC, segregated accounts, and jurisdictional approval—regulatory barriers make this infeasible.

counterarguments.title: An F1=0.98 on 460 clips is statistically fragile when deployed against millions of real-world clips with distribution drift.
counterarguments.evidence_summary: The test set of 460 clips is likely curated, well-lit, and unrepresentative of production footage (low angles, motion blur, outdoor glare, night courts). The 16 false positives and 41 misses suggest the model is still making errors at scale—extrapolated to 1M clips, that's 34,700+ false positives creating noise for customers. Gemini's vision model degrades unpredictably on edge cases, and you have no holdout test set to validate generalization.
counterarguments.recommended_action: Address this risk before investing in: DaaS badge prediction: F1=0.98 (precision=0.99, re

counterarguments.title: White-labeling the Idea Lab assumes these adjacent sports have the same clip volume, annotation infrastructure, and willingness to pay as pickleball—they don't.
counterarguments.evidence_summary: Tennis and badminton have established analytics vendors (Hawk-Eye, Synergy Sports); retail/fashion use computer vision for completely different tasks (SKU detection, not human micro-actions). You'd need to rebuild training data for each sport, retrain models, and sell to buyers who already have solutions. The 'platform play' framing masks that you have zero product-market fit signals outside pickleball, and pivoting dilutes the core DaaS business.
counterarguments.recommended_action: Address this risk before investing in: Idea Lab as a White-Label SaaS

raw_data.target_title: Idea Lab as a White-Label SaaS

counterarguments.title: Adding 5 specific positive examples to a prompt does not systematically fix recall; it's a band-aid that will mask the underlying model limitation and create false confidence.
counterarguments.evidence_summary: You're treating symptoms (5 missed badges) rather than the root cause (Gemini's visual understanding is unreliable on subtle actions like 'rally master'). Adding examples to a prompt inflates F1 on your test set but does nothing for the 50 other badges you haven't caught yet. Once deployed, customers will report new misses weekly, forcing perpetual prompt tweaks. You lack a way to validate whether the fix actually generalizes to unseen clips or just overfits to your 21 examples.
counterarguments.recommended_action: Address this risk before investing in: Prompt fix: 5 badges consistently missed — add pos

raw_data.target_title: Prompt fix: 5 badges consistently missed — add positive examples

counterarguments.title: Existing coaching apps (Tennis.com, EDGE) already have proprietary AI coaches; they have no incentive to rip-and-replace your unproven Idea Lab.
counterarguments.evidence_summary: You're pitching a B2B2C partnership to 50 vendors with strong switching costs and zero leverage. They won't integrate an unvalidated AI coach unless you've proven it drives retention or revenue in their existing cohorts—which requires you to embed pilots at scale (expensive, slow). Meanwhile, OpenAI/Anthropic are shipping their own sports coaching features. This assumes your Idea Lab is differentiated enough to win against those giants, with no evidence of customer traction or benchmark wins.
counterarguments.recommended_action: Address this risk before investing in: Coaching Franchise API

counterarguments.title: A 'facility equity marketplace' requires solving the chicken-egg problem: facilities won't tokenize data without a liquid buyer base, and sponsors won't buy without proof of ROI—neither exists.
counterarguments.evidence_summary: You're proposing a two-sided marketplace (supply: facilities, demand: sponsors) with zero network effects in place. Tokenization adds legal/tax complexity without adding value—facilities care about revenue, not ownership %. Even if you solve that, a 'pickleball content fund' is illiquid and speculative; sponsors want guaranteed impressions or player endorsements, not equity. You'd need to operate the marketplace (legal, settlement, disputes) while bootstrapping DaaS—this dilutes focus and has high operational risk for a 1-person team.
counterarguments.recommended_action: Address this risk before investing in: Facility Equity Marketplace

counterarguments.title: Micro-sponsorship auctions assume players have personal brands worth monetizing and that brands will bid on sub-1K-follower players—neither is true in pickleball.
counterarguments.evidence_summary: Pickleball's creator economy is nascent; most players have no social presence. Brands optimize for macro-influencers and established athletes with legal reps; they won't set up auctions to bid on randomized micro-moments from unknown players. Even if they did, the unit economics are terrible (a brand pays $50 to sponsor a 10-second clip from a player with 200 followers?). This is a creator-economy fantasy, not a revenue model, and it cannibalizes direct sponsorship relationships Courtana could negotiate with brands instead.
counterarguments.recommended_action: Address this risk before investing in: Player-to-Brand Sponsorship Auction

counterarguments.title: Venue-only detections are a data quality issue, but the fix (reporting 'true' product-use) assumes you can reliably distinguish venue branding from product use—you likely can't.
counterarguments.evidence_summary: A JOOLA net and a JOOLA paddle look visually similar to a model; you're conflating two detection problems. If you report only 'true' product-use counts to brands, you'll miss sales attribution (a player practicing at a JOOLA venue *is* exposed to the brand). Brands care about total impression share, not just product-use; filtering out venue data may actually harm the value of your dataset. You also have no way to validate this correction without manually reviewing thousands of clips—operationally infeasible.
counterarguments.recommended_action: Address this risk before investing in: Net/venue branding inflates 7 brands — true share-

raw_data.target_title: Net/venue branding inflates 7 brands — true share-of-court differs from raw counts

counterarguments.title: A simple (quality + watchability) / 2 score assumes these two metrics are equally weighted, linearly additive, and predictive of virality—none of which you've validated.
counterarguments.evidence_summary: You've identified a correlation (top 100 viral clips score higher) but not causation or weighting. Virality also depends on timing, distribution platform, hashtags, and luck—factors your metrics don't capture. A clip could be high-quality but not watchable (slow rally) or watchable but low-quality (blurry but dramatic). Using this score to pitch 'viral prediction' to brands or players sets false expectations; you'll be wrong 40% of the time, damaging credibility. This needs validation on a held-out set and comparison to a null model (e.g., random clips go viral at X% baseline rate).
counterarguments.recommended_action: Address this risk before investing in: Viral formula: quality (7.41 vs 4.74) + watchabili

raw_data.target_title: Viral formula: quality (7.41 vs 4.74) + watchability (6.79 vs 2.78)
